attributes.stem: A 54-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with immense pain in his hands and wrist which started a couple hours ago. He complains of similar episodic pains for many years and describes the occurrence initially as unilateral which then developed into bilateral sudden attacks over time. He has not seen a doctor for more than ten years due to financial problems. He has a medical history of chronic kidney disease from a young age due to congenital kidney malformation. His medications include OTC NSAIDs which help to relieve the pain of acute flares of joint pain. On physical examination multiple painless hard nodules with joint deformities of the hand (Image) and hardened nodules around the pinna of the external ear are noted.
attributes.stemQuestion: What one of the following is the next best step in management for treating the acute pain?
attributes.explanation:
The patient has chronic gout with extensive soft tissue tophi as the crystal deposition is also found in the pinna of the external ear. First-line management in the case of acute gout flare is anti-inflammatory therapy such as colchicine, NSAIDs or glucocorticoids. However, since the patient has a history of chronic kidney disease (CKD), colchicine and NSAIDs, which are nephrotoxic, should be avoided and glucocorticoids (such as prednisolone) should be administered instead.

Cephalexin may be helpful if there is associated infection but the most likely diagnosis in this case is gout.

Morphine is useful for severe pain however not first-line treatment for reducing inflammation and pain in gout, and should be used avoided with patients with CKD.

The next step of management is to initiate urate-lowering therapy (ULT) such as xanthine-oxidase inhibitors like allopurinol or uricosurics like probenecid in order to reduce the incidence of acute flares in future. Allopurinol was initially though to be avoided during acute flares but trials have demonstrated that it should be continued if already started, and can be commenced at a low dose during acute flares. Whilst it may be started in this case, the treatment of choice to relieve the pain and inflammation would be the glucocorticoid. Maintenance aims to target serum urate levels of < 0.36 mmol/L, and < 0.30 mmol/L if there is tophi present and requires frequent checking with titration of ULT to achieved. Testing for HLA-B*5801 allele can be considered for patients of Asian descent and renal impairment due to risk of allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome. Febuxostat can also be considered if allopurinol is contraindicated or there is a risk of AHS.

Lifestyle factors also plays an essential role in management to lower the risk of flares. The patient should be advised to cut down on any alcohol intake, meats, seafood, organ meats, soft drinks and juice). Water, exercise and healthy diet is recommended and weight loss is also highly encouraged. The patient must be screened for cardiovascular disease, kidney disease and diabetes.
learningPoints: Anti-inflammatory agents (NSAIDS, colchicine and glucocorticoids) can be used concurrently with urate-lowering therapy in acute gout flares.
Urate-lowering therapy is the best long-term management of gout with the goals of reducing acute flares and maximising joint function
references: FitzGerald JD, Dalbeth N, Mikuls T, Brignardello-Petersen R, Guyatt G, Abeles AM. 2020 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Management of Gout. Arthritis Care Res (Hoboken). 2020;72(6):744-760. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32391934/

Khanna D, Khanna PP, Fitzgerald JD, Singh MK, Bae S, Neogi T, et al. 2012 American College of Rheumatology guidelines for management of gout. Part 2: therapy and antiinflammatory prophylaxis of acute gouty arthritis. Arthritis Care Res (Hoboken). 2012;64(10):1447-61. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23024029/

Qaseem A, Harris RP, Forciea MA. Management of Acute and Recurrent Gout: A Clinical Practice Guideline From the American College of Physicians. Ann Intern Med. 2016;166(1):58. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27802508/
Robinson P, Stamp L. The Management of Gout: Much has Changed. Australian Family Physician. 2016; 45 (5). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2016/may/the-management-of-gout-much-has-changed
